news_summary: Today's technology news highlights breakthroughs in protein engineering for advanced sensing and computing, innovative flight demonstration projects, and fascinating evolutionary insights from reptiles and animals that deepen our understanding of biology and adaptation.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant les avancées réalisées dans le domaine de l'ingénierie des protéines pour des systèmes de détection et de calcul avancés, des projets innovants de démonstration de vol, ainsi que des découvertes fascinantes sur l'évolution des reptiles et des animaux qui approfondissent notre compréhension de la biologie et de l'adaptation.

articles.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
articles.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.
articles.content:
Evolution is nature's way of engineering biological systems. Inside cells, many variations of DNA, RNA, and proteins arise, and natural selection favors the organisms that function most effectively. Humans began harnessing this process long ago. Early farmers influenced evolution by choosing which crops and livestock reproduced, allowing the most productive plants and animals to pass on their traits.

Today, scientists apply similar principles in the lab through a technique known as directed evolution. Researchers use it to improve proteins such as enzymes and antibodies that play important roles in medicine, industrial manufacturing, and even everyday products like laundry detergents.

Limits of Traditional Directed Evolution

Despite its success, standard directed evolution methods have a key limitation. They usually impose a constant selection pressure that favors proteins that remain highly active all the time. However, real biological systems rarely function this way. Many proteins serve as signals, molecular switches, or "logic gates" (proteins that combine multiple inputs to make a yes-or-no decision), meaning they must change states as conditions shift.

For example, a protein might briefly activate, then turn off, and later switch on again. When evolution experiments only reward a single state, other necessary states can degrade. As a result, proteins may lose the ability to switch properly, which can be harmful for cells (e.g. kill a cell). Because of this challenge, creating proteins with complex multi-state behavior has proven difficult with existing directed evolution approaches.

A Light-Based Strategy for Protein Evolution

Researchers led by Sahand Jamal Rahi at EPFL's Laboratory of the Physics of Biological Systems have introduced a new approach called "optovolution." This method uses light to steer the evolution of proteins that can perform dynamic functions and even carry out simple computational tasks that follow yes-or-no rules.

The study, published in Cell, helps bring directed evolution closer to how cells naturally operate. In living systems, timing and switching between states are just as important as the strength of a signal.

Engineering Yeast Cells to Select the Best Proteins

To build their system, the researchers used the budding yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae, an organism widely used both in brewing and scientific research. They redesigned the yeast cell cycle so that cell division depended on the behavior of the protein being evolved. The protein needed to switch cleanly between active and inactive states for the cell to survive.

The scientists connected the protein's output signal to a regulator that controls the cell cycle. This regulator is essential during one stage but becomes toxic during another. If the protein remained on or off for too long, the yeast cell would stall or die. Only cells containing proteins that switched at the correct time continued to divide.

Using Light to Control Evolution in Real Time

Light provided a way to control this process with precision. The researchers used optogenetics, a technique that activates or deactivates genes using light. By delivering timed pulses of light, they forced the protein to alternate between states.

Each yeast cell cycle lasts about 90 minutes, creating a rapid pass or fail test of whether the protein switched at the correct moment. Proteins that performed best allowed the cell to survive and reproduce, while poorly switching variants were eliminated. This allowed optovolution to automatically select proteins with better dynamic behavior without manual screening or repeated adjustments.

New Protein Variants and Expanded Color Sensitivity

Using optovolution, the team evolved several different types of proteins. They first improved a commonly used light controlled transcription factor. The researchers generated 19 new variants that showed greater sensitivity to light, reduced activity in darkness, or the ability to respond to green light rather than only blue light. Engineering proteins that respond to warmer colors than blue has long been considered extremely difficult because of how these proteins absorb light.

The scientists also evolved a red light optogenetic system so that yeast cells no longer required an added chemical cofactor. Evolution produced a mutation that disabled a normal yeast transport protein. This unexpected change allowed the system to use light sensitive molecules already present inside the cell, making the system easier to use in experiments.

Proteins That Act Like Tiny Computers

The study also demonstrated that optovolution can extend beyond light sensing proteins. The researchers evolved a transcription factor that functions like a single protein computer. It activated genes only when two different inputs appeared at the same time - one light signal and one chemical signal.

Dynamic protein behavior is essential for many biological processes, including sensing environmental changes, making decisions inside cells, and controlling cell division. By enabling these behaviors to evolve continuously within living cells, optovolution offers new possibilities for synthetic biology, biotechnology, and fundamental research.

The technique may help scientists design smarter cellular circuits, create optogenetic tools that respond independently to different colors of light, and better understand how complex protein behaviors arise through evolution.

Other contributors
articles.reasoning: The article describes a novel scientific breakthrough in protein engineering using a new light-guided evolution method called optovolution. This technique enables the creation of proteins with dynamic switching and computational behaviors, overcoming limitations of traditional directed evolution. The impact is broad, advancing synthetic biology and biotechnology with potential applications in medicine, research, and industrial processes. The article provides detailed context and significance of the discovery, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Scientists use light to evolve proteins that switch and compute inside cells
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists often improve proteins by a process called directed evolution, which mimics natural selection in the lab. However, traditional methods mostly create proteins that stay active all the time, while many real proteins need to switch on and off to work properly.

**What happened** – Researchers at EPFL developed a new method called optovolution that uses light to guide protein evolution inside yeast cells. They engineered yeast so that the cells survive only if the proteins switch on and off at the right times. By shining timed pulses of light, the team quickly selected proteins that perform better dynamic switching and even behave like tiny logic gates, turning genes on only when two signals are present.

**Impact** – This method overcomes a challenge in protein engineering by allowing proteins to evolve with complex, multi-state behaviors that mimic natural cell functions. The team created new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors, including green and red, which were hard to achieve before. They also made a protein that works like a simple computer inside cells, opening new ways to design smarter biological tools.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to use optovolution to build advanced cellular circuits and improve optogenetic tools that control cells with light. This could lead to better research methods and new applications in medicine and biotechnology by designing proteins that work more like natural systems.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new light-guided evolution method helps create proteins that switch states and compute inside cells, advancing how scientists engineer living systems.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques utilisent la lumière pour faire évoluer des protéines qui s'activent et calculent dans les cellules
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques améliorent souvent les protéines par un processus appelé évolution dirigée, qui imite la sélection naturelle en laboratoire. Cependant, les méthodes traditionnelles créent surtout des protéines toujours actives, alors que beaucoup de protéines naturelles doivent s'allumer et s'éteindre pour bien fonctionner.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'EPFL ont développé une nouvelle méthode appelée optovolution qui utilise la lumière pour guider l'évolution des protéines dans des cellules de levure. Ils ont modifié la levure pour que les cellules survivent seulement si les protéines s'activent et se désactivent au bon moment. En envoyant des impulsions lumineuses programmées, l'équipe a rapidement sélectionné des protéines avec un meilleur comportement dynamique, capables même de fonctionner comme des portes logiques, activant des gènes seulement quand deux signaux sont présents.

**Impact** – Cette méthode permet de dépasser un défi en ingénierie des protéines en faisant évoluer des protéines avec des comportements complexes à plusieurs états, proches des fonctions naturelles des cellules. L'équipe a créé de nouvelles protéines sensibles à la lumière qui réagissent à différentes couleurs, y compris le vert et le rouge, ce qui était difficile auparavant. Ils ont aussi fabriqué une protéine fonctionnant comme un petit ordinateur dans les cellules, ouvrant la voie à des outils biologiques plus intelligents.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques prévoient d'utiliser l'optovolution pour construire des circuits cellulaires avancés et améliorer les outils optogénétiques qui contrôlent les cellules par la lumière. Cela pourrait améliorer les méthodes de recherche et ouvrir de nouvelles applications en médecine et biotechnologie en concevant des protéines qui fonctionnent mieux comme dans la nature.

**En une phrase** – Une nouvelle méthode d'évolution guidée par la lumière aide à créer des protéines qui changent d'état et calculent dans les cellules, faisant progresser la manière dont les scientifiques conçoivent les systèmes vivants.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos usan luz para evolucionar proteínas que se activan y calculan dentro de las células
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos mejoran proteínas con un proceso llamado evolución dirigida, que imita la selección natural en el laboratorio. Sin embargo, los métodos tradicionales crean principalmente proteínas que están activas todo el tiempo, mientras que muchas proteínas reales necesitan activarse y desactivarse para funcionar bien.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del EPFL desarrollaron un nuevo método llamado optovolution que usa luz para guiar la evolución de proteínas dentro de células de levadura. Modificaron la levadura para que las células sobrevivan solo si las proteínas se activan y desactivan en el momento correcto. Al aplicar pulsos de luz programados, el equipo seleccionó rápidamente proteínas que cambian mejor y hasta funcionan como pequeñas puertas lógicas, activando genes solo cuando hay dos señales presentes.

**Impacto** – Este método supera un reto en la ingeniería de proteínas al permitir que evolucionen con comportamientos complejos y múltiples estados, parecidos a las funciones naturales de las células. Crearon nuevas proteínas sensibles a diferentes colores de luz, incluyendo verde y rojo, algo difícil antes. También hicieron una proteína que funciona como una pequeña computadora dentro de las células, abriendo nuevas formas de diseñar herramientas biológicas más inteligentes.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean usar optovolution para construir circuitos celulares avanzados y mejorar herramientas optogenéticas que controlan las células con luz. Esto podría mejorar los métodos de investigación y crear nuevas aplicaciones en medicina y biotecnología diseñando proteínas que funcionen más como en la naturaleza.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo método de evolución guiada por luz ayuda a crear proteínas que cambian de estado y calculan dentro de las células, avanzando la forma en que los científicos diseñan sistemas vivos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing yeast cell shaped like a delicate orb, illuminated by soft pulses of blue, green, and red light beams that weave around it like threads, with abstract, circuit-like protein structures inside the cell dynamically switching states in harmony, symbolizing light-guided evolution and molecular computation within a living system.

articles.title: About Flight Demonstrations and Capabilities (FDC) Project
articles.summary: The FDC project conducts complex integrated small-scale flight research to validate the benefits of new technologies. By modifying aircraft from FDC’s support fleet, the project enables aggressive, success-oriented flight campaign schedules. While many technologies are at mid-levels of technology readiness, the FDC project supports all phases of technology maturation.   FDC’s support aircraft fleet enables safety chase and in-flight experimental measurements for a variety of […]
articles.content:
NASA’s X-59 quiet supersonic research aircraft lifts off for its first flight Tuesday, Oct. 28, 2025, from U.S. Air Force Plant 42 in Palmdale, California. The aircraft’s first flight marks the start of flight testing for NASA’s Quesst mission, the result of years of design, integration, and ground testing and begins a new chapter in NASA’s aeronautics research legacy. NASA/Lori Losey

The FDC project conducts complex integrated small-scale flight research to validate the benefits of new technologies.

By modifying aircraft from FDC’s support fleet, the project enables aggressive, success-oriented flight campaign schedules. While many technologies are at mid-levels of technology readiness, the FDC project supports all phases of technology maturation.

FDC’s support aircraft fleet enables safety chase and in-flight experimental measurements for a variety of NASA missions.

The project collaborates with academia, industry, and government organizations to leverage flight opportunities, and engages with NASA researchers and university students to bring innovative concepts to flight.

The FDC project operates, sustains, and enhances other national flight research capabilities that enable complex high-risk flight research for both NASA and the aviation industry.

These capabilities are located at NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center at Edwards, California, and includes the Aeronautics Test Data Portal, Flight Loads Laboratory, the Dryden Aeronautical Test Range, and a suite of flight simulators.

The project leverages collaborative opportunities for flight testing from across the aeronautical industry.

Flight Research Facilities

The FDC project validates benefits associated with critical technologies through focused flight experiments. Through the integration of appropriate flight test capabilities and assets — whether from NASA. other government agencies, or industry — FDC campaigns focus on aggressive, success-oriented schedules using the best collection of assets.

The FDC project supports tests of technology at all phases of maturation.

Flight Loads Laboratory

Simulation Lab

Research Aircraft Integration Facility

Dryden Aeronautical Test Range

Support Aircraft and Maintenance Operations

FDC

IASP

Contact Information
articles.reasoning: The article describes NASA's Flight Demonstrations and Capabilities (FDC) project, which supports the development and validation of new aeronautical technologies through integrated flight research. This has broad significance for aviation safety, innovation, and technology maturation, benefiting the general public by advancing aerospace capabilities. The article provides sufficient detail about the project's scope, collaborations, and facilities, highlighting a meaningful technological advancement.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Flight Demonstrations and Capabilities project advances new aviation technologies
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA’s Flight Demonstrations and Capabilities (FDC) project focuses on testing and proving new aviation technologies through real flight experiments. It uses specially modified aircraft and works with partners from universities, industry, and government to improve flight research.

**What happened** – The FDC project carries out small-scale but complex flight tests using a fleet of support aircraft. These planes are changed to meet specific research needs and help complete flight campaigns quickly and safely. The project supports technology development at all stages, from early testing to near-ready products. It also manages important flight research facilities at NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center in California.

**Impact** – This project is important because it helps make sure new aviation technologies actually work when flying. By testing in real conditions, it reduces risks and speeds up the development of safer, better aircraft. The FDC project also supports many NASA missions and helps train future engineers and scientists by involving university students.

**What's next step** – Going forward, the FDC project will continue to support flight tests of emerging technologies and expand partnerships across the aviation industry and research community. This will help bring more innovative ideas from the lab into the sky.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s FDC project plays a key role in safely testing and advancing new aviation technologies through real flight experiments and strong collaborations.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le projet de démonstrations et capacités de vol de la NASA fait progresser les nouvelles technologies aéronautiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le projet de démonstrations et capacités de vol (FDC) de la NASA se concentre sur le test et la validation de nouvelles technologies aéronautiques grâce à des expériences de vol réelles. Il utilise des avions spécialement modifiés et collabore avec des universités, l’industrie et le gouvernement pour améliorer la recherche en vol.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le projet FDC réalise des essais de vol complexes à petite échelle avec une flotte d’avions de soutien. Ces avions sont adaptés aux besoins spécifiques de recherche et permettent de mener des campagnes de vol rapidement et en toute sécurité. Le projet soutient le développement des technologies à toutes les étapes, du test initial aux produits presque prêts. Il gère aussi des installations importantes de recherche en vol au Centre de recherche en vol Armstrong de la NASA en Californie.

**Impact** – Ce projet est important car il vérifie que les nouvelles technologies aéronautiques fonctionnent bien en conditions réelles de vol. En testant sur le terrain, il réduit les risques et accélère le développement d’avions plus sûrs et performants. Le projet FDC soutient aussi de nombreuses missions de la NASA et forme les futurs ingénieurs et scientifiques en impliquant des étudiants universitaires.

**Prochaine étape** – Le projet FDC continuera à soutenir les essais en vol des technologies émergentes et à élargir ses partenariats avec l’industrie aéronautique et la communauté de recherche. Cela permettra de faire passer plus d’idées innovantes du laboratoire au ciel.

**En une phrase** – Le projet FDC de la NASA joue un rôle clé pour tester en toute sécurité et faire avancer les nouvelles technologies aéronautiques grâce à des expériences de vol réelles et des collaborations solides.
articles.personality_title_es: El proyecto de demostraciones y capacidades de vuelo de la NASA impulsa nuevas tecnologías aeronáuticas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El proyecto de demostraciones y capacidades de vuelo (FDC) de la NASA se dedica a probar y validar nuevas tecnologías de aviación mediante experimentos reales de vuelo. Usa aviones modificados especialmente y colabora con universidades, la industria y el gobierno para mejorar la investigación en vuelo.

**Qué pasó** – El proyecto FDC realiza pruebas de vuelo complejas a pequeña escala con una flota de aviones de apoyo. Estos aviones se adaptan a las necesidades específicas de investigación y permiten completar campañas de vuelo de forma rápida y segura. El proyecto apoya el desarrollo tecnológico en todas sus etapas, desde pruebas iniciales hasta productos casi listos. También administra importantes instalaciones de investigación en vuelo en el Centro de Investigación de Vuelo Armstrong de la NASA en California.

**Impacto** – Este proyecto es importante porque asegura que las nuevas tecnologías aeronáuticas funcionen bien en condiciones reales de vuelo. Al probar en el campo, reduce riesgos y acelera el desarrollo de aviones más seguros y eficientes. El proyecto FDC también apoya muchas misiones de la NASA y ayuda a formar a futuros ingenieros y científicos al involucrar a estudiantes universitarios.

**Próximo paso** – El proyecto FDC continuará apoyando pruebas de vuelo de tecnologías emergentes y ampliará sus asociaciones con la industria aeronáutica y la comunidad investigadora. Esto ayudará a llevar más ideas innovadoras del laboratorio al cielo.

**Una frase clave** – El proyecto FDC de la NASA es fundamental para probar de forma segura y avanzar en nuevas tecnologías aeronáuticas mediante experimentos reales de vuelo y sólidas colaboraciones.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a sleek, futuristic supersonic aircraft gently lifting off from a desert runway at NASA’s Flight Research Center, surrounded by symbolic abstract shapes representing collaboration and innovation—interlocking gears and flowing lines connecting small-scale experimental drones and support aircraft—rendered in warm, natural earth tones and soft blues under a clear sky.

articles.title: Ancient 'weirdo' reptile graduated from 4 legs to 2 in adolescence
articles.summary: Sonselasuchus cedrus, discovered in fossils from Arizona, was a crocodile relative from the Triassic period that grew into an ostrich-like adult
articles.content:
Sonselasuchus cedrus, discovered in fossils from Arizona, was a crocodile relative from the Triassic period that grew into an ostrich-like adult

Artist’s reconstruction of Sonselasuchus cedrus in its environment, 215 million years ago Gabriel Ugueto

An early relative of crocodiles spent its juvenile years walking on all fours, then stood up on two legs as an adult.

Its arm and leg bones grew at different rates to enable this transition. “The forelimb starts out like 75 per cent the length of the hind-limb, and then it ends up being more like 50 [per cent],” says Elliott Armour Smith at the University of Washington in Seattle.

Advertisement

The finding adds to evidence that crocodile-like animals in the early dinosaur era were extremely diverse, with some even adopting bodies and lifestyles similar to those of modern ostriches.

With Christian Sidor, also at the University of Washington, Armour Smith excavated Kaye Quarry in Petrified Forest National Park in Arizona. It consists of mudstones and sandstones deposited by a river that flowed about 215 million years ago, in the Triassic period.

Armour Smith and Sidor found more than 3000 bones belonging to early relatives of crocodiles called shuvosaurids. “It’s a jumbled mess of individual limb bones that don’t necessarily have an association between the individual animals,” says Armour Smith.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Earth Edition Unmissable news about our planet, delivered straight to your inbox each month. Sign up to newsletter

Nevertheless, the pair were able to identify a new shuvosaurid, which they called Sonselasuchus cedrus. Over 950 of the bones belonged to this species. It didn’t resemble a modern crocodile, but instead looked more like a flightless bird or theropod dinosaur. Its arms were short and instead of a mouth filled with teeth, it had a toothless beak.

Other shuvosaurids have similar bodies. “Shuvosaurids are these absolute weirdos that live in the late Triassic,” says Michelle Stocker at Virginia Tech. “They really look like dinosaurs.” They most resemble ornithomimids, which were ostrich-like dinosaurs that lived in the late Cretaceous period, more than 100 million years after the shuvosaurids.

Sonselasuchus cedrus seems to have started life walking on all fours. Bones from younger individuals show the front and rear limbs were relatively similar in size. But in older individuals, the hind-limbs grew much more and also showed signs of bearing more weight. “The larger femurs in the population are rather robust,” says Armour Smith, whereas “even the largest humerus is relatively delicate”.

This is unusual, but not unique. A 2019 study found evidence of two dinosaur species transitioning from quadrupedal to bipedal walking as they grew. One was a sauropodomorph, an ancestor of the huge sauropods like Brachiosaurus, and the other was an early ceratopsian distantly related to Triceratops.

It may be that juvenile and adult S. cedrus lived fairly separate lives and even ate different diets, as some crocodilians do today, says Stocker.

Popular descriptions of the dinosaur era often give the impression that dinosaurs, especially birds, were evolving in very creative ways, while crocodiles pretty much stayed the same. That misrepresents the diversity of pseudosuchians – the branch of the reptile tree that includes crocodiles, says Stocker. “They’re actually doing a lot of the really unique, crazy stuff first, and then dinosaurs are picking it up later.”

Journal reference: Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology DOI: 10.1080/02724634.2025.2604859
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant paleontological discovery revealing evolutionary adaptations in ancient crocodile relatives, providing new insights into reptile diversity and evolution during the Triassic period. This contributes meaningful scientific knowledge with broad educational and curiosity-driven impact.
articles.personality_title: Ancient crocodile relative walked on four legs as a child, two legs as an adult
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – About 215 million years ago, during the Triassic period, many strange reptiles lived on Earth. Among them were early relatives of crocodiles, called shuvosaurids, which looked very different from modern crocodiles.

**What happened** – Scientists found fossils in Arizona of a species named Sonselasuchus cedrus. This reptile started life walking on all fours but grew into an adult that walked on two legs, similar to an ostrich. Researchers studied over 950 bones and noticed that the front legs grew slower than the back legs, allowing this change.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that crocodile relatives were more diverse and interesting than previously thought. Sonselasuchus cedrus did not look like a crocodile but more like a bird without teeth. It also suggests young and adult animals might have lived differently and eaten different foods, showing complex behavior long ago.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue studying these fossils and others to learn how common this leg change was and how these animals lived. Understanding their growth and lifestyles can help explain how reptiles evolved before dinosaurs became dominant.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Sonselasuchus cedrus, an ancient crocodile relative, changed from walking on four legs as a juvenile to two legs as an adult, revealing surprising reptile diversity in the Triassic period.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un ancien parent du crocodile marchait à quatre pattes enfant, puis à deux pattes adulte
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Il y a environ 215 millions d’années, durant le Trias, de nombreux reptiles étranges peuplaient la Terre. Parmi eux, des parents anciens des crocodiles, appelés shuvosauridés, qui ressemblaient beaucoup moins aux crocodiles modernes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont découvert des fossiles en Arizona d’une espèce nommée Sonselasuchus cedrus. Ce reptile marchait à quatre pattes quand il était jeune, puis passait à la marche bipède à l’âge adulte, un peu comme une autruche. Les chercheurs ont étudié plus de 950 os et ont remarqué que les membres avant grandissaient moins vite que les membres arrière, ce qui expliquait ce changement.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que les proches parents des crocodiles étaient plus variés et surprenants qu’on le pensait. Sonselasuchus cedrus ne ressemblait pas à un crocodile mais plutôt à un oiseau sans dents. Elle suggère aussi que les jeunes et les adultes pouvaient vivre différemment et avoir des régimes alimentaires variés, montrant un comportement complexe il y a très longtemps.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à étudier ces fossiles et d’autres pour comprendre à quel point ce changement de posture était courant et comment ces animaux vivaient. Comprendre leur croissance et mode de vie aidera à expliquer l’évolution des reptiles avant que les dinosaures ne dominent.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Sonselasuchus cedrus, un ancien parent du crocodile, est passé de la marche à quatre pattes chez le jeune à la marche à deux pattes chez l’adulte, révélant une diversité surprenante des reptiles au Trias.
articles.personality_title_es: Un antiguo pariente del cocodrilo caminaba en cuatro patas de niño y en dos patas de adulto
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Hace unos 215 millones de años, en el período Triásico, muchos reptiles extraños vivían en la Tierra. Entre ellos estaban los primeros parientes de los cocodrilos, llamados shuvosáuridos, que eran muy diferentes a los cocodrilos modernos.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos encontraron fósiles en Arizona de una especie llamada Sonselasuchus cedrus. Este reptil caminaba con cuatro patas cuando era joven y luego creció para caminar con dos patas, parecido a un avestruz. Los investigadores estudiaron más de 950 huesos y notaron que las patas delanteras crecían más lento que las traseras, lo que permitió este cambio.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que los parientes de los cocodrilos eran más variados e interesantes de lo que se pensaba. Sonselasuchus cedrus no parecía un cocodrilo, sino un ave sin dientes. También sugiere que los jóvenes y adultos podrían haber vivido de manera diferente y comido distintos alimentos, mostrando comportamientos complejos hace mucho tiempo.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán estudiando estos y otros fósiles para entender qué tan común era este cambio de caminar con cuatro patas a dos patas y cómo vivían estos animales. Entender su crecimiento y estilo de vida puede ayudar a explicar cómo evolucionaron los reptiles antes de que los dinosaurios dominaran.

**Resumen en una frase** – Sonselasuchus cedrus, un antiguo pariente del cocodrilo, pasó de caminar en cuatro patas de joven a dos patas de adulto, mostrando una sorprendente diversidad de reptiles en el Triásico.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a prehistoric riverbank scene 215 million years ago, featuring a graceful, ostrich-like Sonselasuchus cedrus standing upright on two strong hind legs with shorter forelimbs, surrounded by scattered fossilized bones partially embedded in mudstone and sandstone, rendered in soft natural earth tones.

articles.title: 'Singing' dogs may show the evolutionary roots of musicality
articles.summary: Some Samoyeds adjust the pitch of their howls depending on the music being played, showing a form of vocal ability they might have inherited from their wolf ancestors
articles.content:
Some Samoyeds adjust the pitch of their howls depending on the music being played, showing a form of vocal ability they might have inherited from their wolf ancestors

Some Samoyed dogs howl along to music Photography by Adri / Alamy

Scroll through #SingingDog hashtags on social media, and you will find many clips of hounds howling along to their favourite tunes. But can a canine Callas show any real musicality?

That’s the question that psychologist Aniruddh Patel at Tufts University in Massachusetts and his colleagues have attempted to answer with an ear-catching new study, which shows that some doggy divas really are perceiving pitch and adjusting their vocals accordingly.

The researchers were inspired by descriptions of wolves’ howling in a pack. “Howling has some similarities to human singing, in that these are long, sustained vocalisation,” says Patel. Observations from the wild suggest that each individual wolf attempts to hit a different tone. The result is a discordant chorus that might create the illusion of a much larger group, which would help to intimidate potential predators. “And so it had been theorised by some wolf biologists that they were actually paying attention and changing their pitch,” he says.

This is very hard to test experimentally, of course, which is why Patel and his colleagues turned to house pets. They asked a handful of dog owners to record their hound’s harmonies to their personally preferred track – first in the original key, and then in versions that had been transposed three semitones above and three semitones below – to see how they would respond to the change in pitch.

The researchers focused their analyses on two ancient breeds – Samoyeds and shiba inus – that are thought to be more closely related to their wolf ancestors than modern varieties. To increase the statistical reliability of the analysis, each dog had to produce at least 30 howls, lasting at least 1 second each, for each of the transposed versions.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Earth Edition Unmissable news about our planet, delivered straight to your inbox each month. Sign up to newsletter

All four of the Samoyeds studied showed some sensitivity to the track’s pitch, consistently adapting their vocalisations to the new key, though in no cases did they match it exactly.

“They’re trying to have some relationship to what they’re hearing with their own voice; they’re not just being triggered to unleash some instinctive and inflexible response,” says Patel. You can see Luna, one of the participants, performing Lady Gaga and Bradley Cooper’s song “Shallow” here:

The two shiba inus, in contrast, appeared to be tone-deaf. “It’s possible there’s some genetic variation within ancient breeds, making some more predisposed to howling,” Patel hypothesises – though he admits he might have found more musicality in a larger sample.

The findings might offer some insights into the origins of human music. Some theorists have argued that singing evolved from the fine motor control that comes with speech, which allows us to mimic complex sounds, but the fact that dogs can also control pitch without any other forms of vocal learning suggests that language would not have been a necessary precursor. “It’s possible that our ability and desire to coordinate pitch with others when we sing has very ancient evolutionary roots, and may not just be a byproduct of our ability to imitate complex sounds,” says Patel.

Exactly why dogs feel the need to join in is another question. “From the videos that we watched, it seems like the dogs are really quite engaged with the music,” he says. They weren’t simply looking at their owner for a reward, but gazing intently into the distance. “So I think [the music] is sort of a surrogate howl-like signal to them, and gets them in this social mode of wanting to join in.”

Buddhamas Pralle Kriengwatana at KU Leuven in Belgium, who recently co-authored a review article examining the evidence for musical appreciation in non-human animals, is intrigued by the findings, though she would like to see more varied samples. “It would have been nice for them to have a comparison of ancient versus modern breeds,” she says.

Regarding the dogs’ somewhat discordant efforts, she suggests there may be many reasons for not matching the exact pitch of the sounds. “Who knows, maybe they want their voices to be heard, and to hear themselves singing,” she says.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a scientific study revealing that some ancient dog breeds can adjust their howling pitch in response to music, indicating an evolutionary root of musicality. This discovery offers meaningful insights into animal cognition and the origins of human music, representing a significant breakthrough in understanding vocal abilities beyond humans. The study has broad scientific and societal interest, with sufficient detail on methodology and implications.
articles.personality_title: Some dogs change their howls to match music, hinting at ancient roots of singing
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been curious if animals like dogs can actually change the sounds they make to follow music, similar to singing. Wolves howl together in ways that might help them sound bigger and scare threats, which inspired researchers to study dogs' howling.

**What happened** – Researchers at Tufts University studied two old dog breeds, Samoyeds and shiba inus. They played songs to these dogs in different pitches and recorded how the dogs howled. Four Samoyeds changed the pitch of their howls to match the music, though not perfectly. The two shiba inus did not adjust their howls.

**Impact** – This shows some dogs can control their howling pitch based on what they hear, not just howling automatically. It suggests that musical ability might come from very old animal traits shared with wolves. This challenges ideas that singing only evolved from human speech skills.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to study more dogs, including modern breeds, to see how common this ability is. They also want to understand why dogs join in music—whether it’s social or just a response to sound.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Some ancient dog breeds can change the pitch of their howls to match music, offering clues about the deep history of singing in animals.
articles.personality_title_fr: Certains chiens modifient leurs hurlements selon la musique, révélant des racines anciennes du chant
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques se demandent si des animaux comme les chiens peuvent modifier leurs sons pour suivre la musique, un peu comme chanter. Les loups hurlent ensemble de façon à paraître plus grands et effrayer les ennemis, ce qui a inspiré l'étude des hurlements des chiens.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'université Tufts ont étudié deux anciennes races de chiens, les Samoyèdes et les shiba inus. Ils ont joué des chansons à différentes hauteurs de notes et enregistré les hurlements des chiens. Quatre Samoyèdes ont changé la hauteur de leurs hurlements pour suivre la musique, sans être exacts. Les deux shiba inus ne l'ont pas fait.

**Impact** – Cela montre que certains chiens peuvent contrôler la hauteur de leurs hurlements selon ce qu'ils entendent, et pas seulement hurler automatiquement. Cela suggère que la capacité musicale vient de traits très anciens partagés avec les loups. Cela remet en question l'idée que le chant vient seulement de la parole humaine.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent étudier plus de chiens, y compris des races modernes, pour savoir à quel point cette capacité est fréquente. Ils veulent aussi comprendre pourquoi les chiens chantent avec la musique : est-ce un comportement social ou une simple réaction au son ?

**Résumé en une phrase** – Certaines anciennes races de chiens peuvent modifier la hauteur de leurs hurlements pour suivre la musique, offrant des indices sur l'histoire ancienne du chant chez les animaux.
articles.personality_title_es: Algunos perros cambian el tono de sus aullidos según la música, mostrando raíces antiguas del canto
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos se han preguntado si animales como los perros pueden cambiar los sonidos que hacen para seguir la música, parecido a cantar. Los lobos aúllan juntos para sonar más grandes y asustar a otros, lo que inspiró estudiar a los perros.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Tufts estudiaron dos razas antiguas de perros, los Samoyedos y shiba inus. Reprodujeron canciones en diferentes tonos y grabaron cómo aullaban los perros. Cuatro Samoyedos ajustaron el tono de sus aullidos para seguir la música, aunque no de forma perfecta. Los dos shiba inus no cambiaron sus aullidos.

**Impacto** – Esto muestra que algunos perros pueden controlar el tono de sus aullidos según lo que escuchan, no solo aullar por instinto. Sugiere que la habilidad musical viene de rasgos muy antiguos compartidos con los lobos. Esto cuestiona la idea de que el canto solo evolucionó a partir del habla humana.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren estudiar más perros, incluidas razas modernas, para saber qué tan común es esta habilidad. También quieren entender por qué los perros se unen a la música: si es un comportamiento social o solo una reacción al sonido.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Algunas razas antiguas de perros pueden cambiar el tono de sus aullidos para seguir la música, dando pistas sobre la historia antigua del canto en los animales.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two ancient dog breeds—a fluffy Samoyed and a Shiba Inu—howling together beneath a softly glowing moon, their vocal waves illustrated as gentle, colorful sound ribbons intertwining in the air, symbolizing musical pitch and ancestral connection, set against a simple natural background with muted earth tones.
